text: The video shows the robotic repair of a large paraesophageal hernia with a Toupee fundoplication. Eight millimeter robotic trocars are placed, including a liver retractor in the subxiphoid space. The stomach is visible protruding through the crura, with the liver and diaphragm also in view. The herniated stomach is initially reduced from the chest, revealing chronic irritation and thickening at the diaphragmatic crura, particularly on the anterior portion.

The surgeon opens the pars flaccida to expose the right crus of the diaphragm, deflating the stomach with an orogastric tube. The peritoneum overlying the right crus is opened to access the hernia sac, leaving some peritoneum intact for closure. The underlying muscle is visualized, and dissection continues over the anterior diaphragmatic hiatus, freeing the hernia sac from surrounding structures, including the lung pleura. The dissection extends to the left side, creating a window and exposing the left crus. The greater curvature of the stomach is opened and extended to the left crux of the diaphragm. The hernia sac is visible on the anterior gastroesophageal junction, with views of the right and left crura and the esophagus through the diaphragmatic hiatus. The hernia sac is dissected off the anterior stomach onto the esophagus for removal.

The anterior vagus nerve is visualized and carefully preserved, as is the posterior vagus nerve. The hernia sac is removed almost entirely and extracted from the abdomen. A Penrose drain is placed around the gastroesophageal junction and secured with an endo loop for retraction. The surgeon performs a 360-degree dissection around the esophagus within the mediastinum to achieve sufficient laxity of the gastroesophageal junction below the diaphragmatic hiatus.

The surgeon dissects posterior to the esophagus, revealing chronic scarring within the mediastinum. The aorta, right and left pleura, and esophagus are visible at the top of the screen. The gastroesophageal junction is positioned more than three centimeters below the diaphragmatic hiatus, indicating sufficient laxity without downward tension. Sutures are introduced into the surgical field.

The surgeon performs hiatal closure using a unidirectional barbed permanent suture, taking large bites of the crura. The closure is sized to accommodate a large grasping forcep, ensuring sufficient laxity. The fundoplication is initiated by bringing the fundus posterior to the esophagus and performing a shoeshine maneuver. Three sutures are placed from the stomach to the hiatal closure, forming a toupee fundoplication.

The surgeon performs a 270-degree partial wrap, known as a Toupee fundoplication, using slip knots to achieve tension on the sutures. Permanent sutures are placed from the esophagus to the stomach at the ten o'clock position, carefully avoiding the anterior vagus nerves. Additional sutures are placed from the stomach to the esophagus at the two o'clock position, and a final suture is placed from the left cruce to the stomach posteriorly.

The surgeon cuts and removes the Penrose drain, followed by the removal of needles and the Penrose drain from the abdomen. The wrap is visualized, and the liver retractor is removed, marking the completion of the procedure.
